videos.title: 20 Essential Tips for a First Visit to Washington, DC
videos.description:
The seat of American government, bustling Washington DC is home to the touchstone by which all other museums are judged as well as monuments and memorials that tell the story of the nation’s rich history.

A DC trip can be easier on the budget than other destinations across the country because so many attractions within the city are free. 

If you plan on spending time in the District and you’ve never been here before, we’ve put together a list just for you. In this episode, we share with you 20 essential tips for a first visit to Washington DC. 

#WashingtonDC #DC #TravelTips

🌐 GALLIVANTER WEBSITE/BLOG.
https://chadgallivanter.com/blog/dc

📬 MAIL.
Chad Gallivanter
P.O. Box 636
Lake Helen, FL 32744

📱 SOCIAL.
TWITTER: http://twitter.com/ChadGallivanter
INSTAGRAM: http://instagram.com/ChadGallivanter
FACEBOOK: http://facebook.com/ChadGallivanter
tags: what to do in washington dc
things to do in washington dc
videos.transcript: [Music] the seat of american government bustling washington dc is home to the touchstone by which all other museums are judged as well as monuments and memorials that tell the story of the nation's rich history a dc trip can be easier on the budget than other destinations across the country because so many attractions within the city are free if you plan on spending time in the district and you've never been here before we've put together a list just for you in today's video we're going to share with you 20 essential tips for a first visit to washington dc ways to get around landmarks and museums where advanced tickets are required in the best neighborhoods you should explore to get away from the crowds i promise you you don't want to miss these tips all of that and so much more straight ahead from the nation's capital [Music] a trip to washington d.c is great any time of the year but most would agree springtime is one of the best seasons to visit the national cherry blossom festival takes place from mid-march through mid-april every year with a full calendar of cultural events held around the city peak bloom for the cherry blossom can be difficult to accurately predict however the national park service keeps the public updated on its website and can usually have an estimate about 10 days out when it comes to driving in dc the best piece of advice i can give you is don't do it take the easiest route to the place you're staying park your car and forget it during the duration of your time here with all the one-way streets that fan out in all different directions it's best to leave the driving to someone else washington has an excellent public transportation system including the metro subway and buses both are popular with locals and visitors alike and should be utilized if you really want a successful trip the metro features six color-coded lines that connect with each other at transfer stations the metro services the district as well as suburban virginia and maryland fares range from a dollar for seniors and riders with qualifying disabilities to peak fares as much as six dollars the website on your screen is the best resource to learn about the current fair schedule there are also single and multiple day unlimited passes regular routes for metro bus have a two dollar cost to ride while you'll pay 425 for an express route be aware something called a smart trip card is needed to enter and exit the system we have links to where you can learn more about the rechargeable card including how and where to purchase along with information about the smartphone app at our website plenty of activities in dc that make it so easy to stay within your vacation budget the 17 museums galleries and zoo that make up the smithsonian institution have free entry visiting at least a couple of these remarkable educational resources should be part of any trip to the nation's capital while the dc metro is awesome for getting you around the city it does not service the national mall the museums and landmarks along this expansive green space are better experienced on foot so be sure to pack comfortable shoes you will be doing a lot of walking in the district but let me also recommend this the dc circulator is a different public bus system from metro bus and provides several distinct routes across the city including around the national mall it makes 17 stops along the route with buses scheduled to arrive every 10 minutes saving you precious time and energy best of all rides are only a buck with seniors and persons with disabilities only charged 50 cents per ride the first time abby and i paid a visit to washington dc we spent three nights and we just about killed ourselves exploring most of the landmarks in the district i still don't know how we did it but the metro was definitely a lifesaver for a first time visitor to d.c it's important to lower expectations you need to have a list of the places you definitely want to hit try to stick to it and realize you won't be able to see every landmark or visit every museum in one vacation it's okay though saving things to see and do for the next visit is always a good thing going through security checks is the norm around washington almost all attractions and museums have some type of security to go through with some a lot more stringent than others you'll be passing through a metal detector at many of the museums whatever you do don't bring mace or pepper spray with you to any museum in dc they're considered weapons one of the most emotional places you'll visit in dc is the national archives museum inside this building that houses more than three billion records are the three most important documents in american history the declaration of independence the u.s constitution and the bill of rights these can be viewed in the building's rotunda for the charters of freedom many people report needing a moment after seeing the documents in person so bring your hanky entry to the national archives is free want to tour the white house during your visit plan ahead as far in advance as possible white house tours can be arranged by contacting your member of congress public tours are typically available from 8 am until 12 30 pm tuesday wednesday thursday friday and saturday excluding federal holidays or unless otherwise noted the tours are subject to change with little notice but they are free of charge advance reservations are now required to tour the u.s capitol building available monday through friday 9 a.m until 3 p.m tours are led by professional guides and include the rotunda national statuary hall and the crypt the tour does not include the senate and house galleries [Music] the world's largest collection of books is located across from the capitol at the library of congress in a city of impressive buildings this is the one most likely to leave your jaw on the floor plus the large gift shop has an impressive collection of books and unique souvenirs you can take back home experiencing the nation's capital city should not be limited to just the famous landmarks and museums factor in some time to explore some of the district's diverse neighborhoods we love popping in to some of the galleries boutiques and eateries near dupont circle georgetown is also a favorite known for its cobblestone sidewalks and gorgeous architecture along with upscale home design stores we always make it a point to grab a photo of the friendship archway in chinatown and head up to u street where we indulge at the original location of ben's chili bowl just be prepared for a line out the door [Music] ranked as one of the most literate cities in the nation bibliophiles love dc for its independent and locally owned bookstores so when you're checking out neighborhoods like dupont circle make it a point to find a couple of good reads at second story books or cramers we have links to some of our favorite bookstores at chatgallivanter.com [Music] while there's no admission fee for any of the dc smithsonian some require timed passes to save visitors from waiting in long lines this applies to the national museum of african american history and culture the u.s holocaust memorial museum and the national zoo you can find out more by visiting the websites of each location we have links at our website the major monuments like the lincoln memorial jefferson memorial and washington monument are iconic and hard to miss because of their placement but some of the smaller newer memorials and monuments should not be overlooked the fdr memorial is on a 7.5 acre more secluded plot along the southwest side of the tidal basin dedicated in 2011 the mlk memorial is located at the northwest corner of the tidal basin on a sight line connecting the lincoln memorial to the northwest and the jefferson memorial to the southeast the black granite walls of the vietnam memorial are within constitution gardens adjacent to the national mall and a short walk from the lincoln memorial these memorials tend to blend into their surroundings more and their sometimes easy to pass by unless you plan ahead and know where to look your first time in dc should include a visit to arlington national cemetery these hallowed grounds across the potomac river from downtown are where the heroes who served in the nation's military are laid to rest it's also where you'll find the tomb of the unknowns and the gravesite of president kennedy and those of his brothers bobby and teddy the easiest way to get to arlington is by taking the metro blue line there's a station on memorial avenue just before the cemetery's main entrance search out more than a hundred gargoyles and grotesques at the washington national cathedral this neo-gothic building looks old but was actually constructed in the 20th century sightseeing hours vary and there normally is an admission charge check our website for a link to information about the cathedral the collection of theaters known as the kennedy center is the nation's busiest performing arts center free guided tours of the campus are offered daily and on select days you might be able to catch free film screenings or a live music concert and don't miss the spectacular views of the city from the rooftop terrace we have a link to the kennedy center on our website [Music] a lot of good food options in one place can be found at union market this gourmet food hall has become dc's hippest feasting ground a former mid-century produce and meat-selling bazaar that today is occupied by food stalls informal restaurants and kitchenware boutiques established by an act of congress in 1927 the national arboretum covers nearly 450 acres and is free to visit the grounds here are visually captivating almost any time of the year but really come alive during the spring time on site you'll find the national capital columns the same set of corinthian columns that once supported the east portico of the capitol building [Music] founding father and the nation's first president george washington called this estate overlooking the potomac home mount vernon is open daily 365 days a year capacity is limited and tours require a timed tour ticket for guaranteed entry it's recommended you purchase your tickets in advance online [Music] that's it our list of 20 essential tips for a first visit to washington dc what would you add to the list let us know in the comments we always love hearing from you do you enjoy this type of content we'd love to have you sign up for our free newsletter gallivanter75 where we provide quick tips and photos from some of our favorite places sign up now free at our website chadgalavanter as always thank you for watching from the nation's capital see you next time [Music] [Music] you

videos.title: A Simple Guide to the DC Metro
videos.description:
David explains the Washington DC Metro, it's history, map, payment system, etiquette, and then takes us on a demo trip. 

Learn about the world from your favorite creators https://www.brighttrip.com/

TIMESTAMPS
00:00 Overview and History
01:07 The Map
05:36 Navigation Apps
06:32 Payment
08:23 Demo Trip
11:43 Rules of the Rail

SOCIALS
Bright Trip Newsletter: https://mailchi.mp/brighttrip.com/signup-form
Bright Trip on IG: https://www.instagram.com/bright.trip/
Bright Trip on Pinterest: https://www.pinterest.com/brighttriptravel/
Bright Trip on Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/BrightTripTeam
Bright Trip on Twitter: https://twitter.com/trip_bright

ABOUT BRIGHT TRIP
Bright Trip creates video-based travel courses to help you travel smarter. From our location courses that visually demystify places like Tokyo, London, Costa Rica, or Cape Town to our skills courses that cover how to travel solo, with kids, or how to document your travels more effectively and efficiently - each course is created by real travelers, like you, and aims to create a community of curious travelers that are eager to travel smarter. 

CREDITS
Executive Producer: Andrew McGarrity
Script: Driaan Louw, David Gordon
Host + Camera: David Gordon
Post: Driaan Louw

Music: MusicBed
https://www.musicbed.com/

For business inquiries: Partnerships@BrightTrip.com

#brighttrip #travelguide #travelsmarter
videos.transcript: 1 2 If you're visiting DC, the Metro is one of the best ways to get in and out of the city. Every weekday, 475,000 people ride the Metro. In the city center, lines run underground and then rise up to extend in every direction, covering DC, Maryland, and Virginia. The DC Metro is probably most famous for its striking architecture, originally designed by architect Harry Weese. Strong and minimal lines work together with exposed concrete, uniform [music] materials, and dramatic indirect lighting to create an experience unmatched by any other system in the US. Along with the architecture, the wayfinding signage was designed by Massimo Vignelli, who is famous for designing New York subway signage and map. Construction for the Metro began in 1969. Seven years later, in 1976, the first public service ran. This makes the DC Metro about 70 years younger than the New York subway, [music] and that age gap can be felt in the generally cleaner, smoother ride as you zip around the city. The DC Metro works similar to most major systems around the world. But if you're new to the wonders of the public rail, or you just need [music] a primer, then you are in the right place. So, let's take a look at the map. [music] This map right here, I got at Metro station. I just went up to the information booth and I asked. You could do that if you like physical maps. I do. Personally, the Metro consists of six color-coded lines: red, blue, orange, yellow, green, and silver. So, I have a couple of friends here to help us guide us through the DC Metro. I have a little ducky. I have a fly. And there was a cat, but the cat's gone now. In some places, lines will share the same track at different times. For instance, the yellow line runs parallel to the green line and the blue line at different intersections. These white dots represent stations. Dots with a double stroke represent transfer stations. You can transfer between lines at share a track at any station and at a transfer station you can switch between lines that intersect. The Red Line is the busiest line in the system running in a U shape from Shady Grove to Glenmont passing through downtown DC. It serves major stations such as Metro Center, [music] Union Station, and Gallery Place Chinatown. If you're coming in from New York, two great ways to get into the city are via bus or train. Both will drop you off at Union Station which is located right here. You could see here a little picture of the capital and it says National Mall. The National Mall covers this whole area. Union Station is just north of the capital. It's about a 15-minute walk to the capital from Union Station and to the rest of the National Mall. If you came in from Ronald Reagan National Airport, which mostly we call DCA in DC, [music] you would take the Yellow Line to the center of the city. But if you're coming from Washington Dulles, then you would take the Silver Line to get to the heart of the National Mall. Smithsonian Station will put you within walking distance of the big museums and the Lincoln Memorial. If you want to cover the mall on foot, the DC Circulator bus starts at Union Station and circles the entire mall including the Tidal Basin. This bus is a part of the DC Metro system so keep this in mind before looking for sightseeing buses that take you around the same area. Farragut West and McPherson Square will get you as close as possible to the northern end of the White House. And located just outside of DC, you could take [music] the Blue Line to visit the Arlington Cemetery. There's a beautiful view of DC from there. [music] DC at with most metros, this map is condensed and it's warped to give you an overview of the system. If you want to get a sense of how far it is to walk from Union Station to the Smithsonian, for example, I recommend using an app like Google Maps or Citymapper. I also recommend using an app in general for riding the metro. All the trains, along with any delays or complications, are updated in real time. So, all you need to do is enter your start and end destination, and your phone will recommend different routes based on time, distance, price, and number of transfers. Once you select your route, all you need to do is follow the instructions, easy peasy. But, if it's not looking so easy peasy, don't stress. That's why we're here. We're going to do a demo trip. It's going to be a lot of fun. Even though these apps can seem wonderful, they're not all-knowing yet. So, make sure to pay attention to the announcements on the screens in case of delays. To use the metro, you need one of these. This is a smart card. It kind of works like a debit card, and because we live in the future, this card is also available digitally, which you can access by downloading the SmartTrip app on your phone. A lot of people use this app, but I have heard negative reviews about the setup process, but once you have everything set up, it works fine. Plastic cards can be purchased at vending machines. You simply press the select to buy a card, and then you can select how much money you want to load onto the card. The DC Metro charges based on distance. At the time of shooting this video, the cheapest fare is $2 and can go up to $6 if you ride it as far as possible. On weekends and weekdays after 9:30 p.m., the ride costs a flat fee of $2. I would say between 10 and $20 is a good start if you're going to be in town for a couple of days. You can use cash or contactless cards to pay. There are also unlimited passes available. Again, if you're only in town for a couple of days, then you should be good with loading money straight on. [music] Note that the card itself costs $2. So, if you use a digital card, you don't have to pay that fee. A quick take it or leave it tip is to check on top of the vending machine. Sometimes people leave them there cuz they're leaving town, they don't need the cards anymore, and they just leave them there for other people to use. To get a digital card on your phone, download the SmarTrip app. Your device does need to have NFC, which is the contactless payment technology. Once you have either a plastic or digital card, you can use it to tap in and out of the fare gates when you want to get on or off the metro. Once you tap out, the system will deduct your fare out of your card depending on how far you've traveled. And another great thing about the SmarTrip card is that you could use it to tap in and out of buses. Works the same way as the metro stations. [music] Okay, so let's put this knowledge to the test. This is my friend Alex. Let's make sure we [music] see your pretty face. And we're going to get her to the airport. [music] Okay, so let's see what Google Maps is telling us. It's giving us some options. We're going to go with the red to the yellow. We are at Cleveland Park right now. We're going to take the red line down to Gallery Place-Chinatown, and then we're going to transfer to the yellow and green line and take that to Ronald Reagan National Airport. [music] This is our red line train to Tacoma. I think this is it, right? Right now we're on the red line headed towards Tacoma. We're going to get off at Gallery Place and transfer to the yellow and green lines. Good job. Okay, we just got off at Gallery Place-Chinatown. This is our transfer station, so we're going to look at the signs pointing towards the yellow and green line. At each station, you have tracks going in opposite directions. So, look at the signs on the side that you're on, and that'll tell you which way the train is going. If you happen to be on the wrong side, you could always take the escalator up and over to get to the right track. So, we're at the yellow and green platform, and we are looking for the side that is taking us to Huntington. And it tells you the color of the line and the last stop of the line of the direction that you're going in. So, we're looking for Huntington, which happens to be right there. So, these signs tell you the color of the line, the direction, and how long. And now, our train has arrived. So, let's get on this train and go to the airport. [music] The DC Metro has these cool digital screens to tell you your stops. You can see we are right now at Archives. We are taking this line all the way to Ronald Reagan National Airport, aka DCA. Another thing is that besides these digital screens, you also have these stops over here. Also, you have these signs in the middle of the car that tell you the next train stop. This is one of the newer DC Metro trains. Uh some of the older ones have carpet and fabric seats, which is pretty cool. This definitely feels cleaner, but those feel homier somehow. So, we're about two stops now from the airport. How do you feel about your flight? Uh I'm pretty excited. What are you going to do in Seattle? Uh drink lots of coffee, maybe go on the Frasier tour. That's the airport right back there. Look how simple that was. Look how easy that was to get to the airport using the Metro. Mission complete. We got Alex to the airport and this is sadly where we say goodbye. I hope you have a good flight, Alex. [music] It's been so much fun telling you about the DC Metro and before I say goodbye, I want to leave you with a couple of guidelines that transplants and locals alike appreciate. A few rules of the rail, if you will. First and foremost, while [music] riding the escalator, please stand on the right and pass on the left. I'm going to run that back. Stand on the right and pass on the left. When you're tapping in or out of a station, make sure you have your card ready as you're approaching the fare gate, so that way you don't become [music] the bottleneck of the congestion. Those lines can get pretty bad and you you don't want to be that person that's holding everybody up. And that's my spiel about the DC Metro. I hope it's helpful. And if you're curious about more DC content, you should check out this video. And before I buzz off, I just want to say that I love you. I love you.

videos.title: 9 Essential Travel Tips for Washington DC Visitors
videos.description:
If you're planning a trip to visit Washington, DC make sure to watch this video for essential travel tips from a local tour guide.

****************************************
~Trip Hacks DC resources for your trip~
Subscribe to the channel! - http://bit.ly/TripHacksDC  
Sign up for a guided tour - http://www.triphacksdc.com/tours 
Trip Hacks DC PODCAST - https://www.triphacksdc.com/podcast
Recommended Hotels Guide - https://www.triphacksdc.com/hotels
Contact us about tours - https://www.triphacksdc.com/contact-us 

****************************************
~Resources from this video~
Where to stay (hotels) podcast episode - https://www.triphacksdc.com/podcast-where-to-stay/
50 things to do in DC - https://youtu.be/goBGO9MyKbg
Metro video playlist - https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=e2c4JJ3emLg&list=PLouaLJ0cMYR3u57Rjxj5vqt2D9AEJMPsS
Local DC tours - https://www.triphacksdc.com/local-washington-dc-tours/

****************************************
~Follow Trip Hacks DC on social media~
https://www.instagram.com/triphacksdc  
https://www.facebook.com/triphacksdc
https://www.tiktok.com/@triphacksdc

****************************************
~Video Chapters~
0:00 Washington DC essential travel tips
0:38 Tip 1: Choose a strategic hotel
2:12 Tip 2: Prioritize!
3:06 Tip 3: Prepare your body
4:31 Tip 4: Know your transportation options
5:51 Tip 5: Get over sticker shock
6:51 Tip 6: Know which sites require tickets and reservations
8:03 Tip 7: Take tours
9:14 Tip 8: Make evening plans
10:05 Tip 9: Pack for the season
tags: things to do in washington dc
what to do in washington dc
videos.transcript: I've got nine essential travel tips that I want to share with everyone who's planning to visit Washington DC but first hello my name is Rob I'm a tour guide and founder of tripex DC tours on this channel you will find videos with my best tips tricks and travel hacks and I host the trip hack DC podcast with in-depth discussions on topics important to visitors if you've ever visited Washington DC before and have any of your own essential travel tips leave it down in the comments so that we can learn from each other My First Essential tip is to choose a strategic hotel now when I say a strategic Hotel it doesn't necessarily need to be a fancy hotel nor does it have to be an expensive hotel a strategic hotel is one that's in a great location close to all of the things that you want to do a strategic hotel is close to Metro and public transportation so that it's really easy for you to get around a strategic hotel is your home base a place where you can go back in the middle of the day to rest and recharge in between activities what happens when people don't choose a strategic hotel is that they waste a ton of time on commuting or they wind up spending a ton of money on things like Taxi and Ubers and wiping out any cost savings that they thought they were getting by not choosing a strategic hotel in the first place if budget is a concern a strategic hotel might be one that includes something like a free breakfast Hotel Brands like Embassy Suits and residents in offer this and there are plenty of them in DC if you need help with this or are feeling overwhelmed I have a hotels guide over on my website tripx d ./ hotels and a podcast episode which I will link to in the description where I discuss the pros and cons of all the areas that I recommend in great detail essential travel tip number two is prioritize according to the Washington DC visitation and impact report the average length of a domestic trip here is only 2.6 nights that's barely enough time to even scratch the surface of what there is to see and do here last year I published a video with 50 things you can do and that's not even a comprehensive list so with relatively little time you simply can't do it all you have to prioritize and make tradeoffs you have to decide what's important to you and what are you okay skipping or saving for a future trip don't choose things because they're on somebody else's bucket list choose things that you want to do and that will make for a memorable trip essential trip number three is to prepare your body for most Washington DC visitors if you are physically able you are going to do a lot of walking if you pick a strategic Hotel you're going to be walking to and from where you're staying you're going to be walking around to see the outdoor sights you're going to be walking around inside the the huge National Mall museums according to the Mayo Clinic the average American walks between 3,000 and 4,000 steps per day on a day when I give a tour I can easily break 15,000 steps on a day when I give two tours I can get up to 25,000 steps and on the busiest days of the year I will walk over 30,000 steps fortunately I can do it because my body is used to it and I do it all the time but if that's not you you need to get prepared whether that's going to your local park and walking laps with your dog or busting out the treadmill you bought during Co this is also why I am so adamant that you must have comfortable walking shoes but comfortable walking shoes alone won't save you you need to break them in and start getting used to them before Before you depart for your trip essential tip number four is to know your transportation options one of my most strongly held beliefs as a tour guide is that Washington DC tourists should not be driving around in cars I know that for a lot of visitors especially Americans driving is literally the only Transportation option that they know but driving around here is stressful frustrating and unpleasant if anything trying to do it will only make your trip worse the good news is DC has plenty of Transportation options I personally get around primarily on foot on bike on Metro and on bus and even though I rarely use them it is very good to know that a taxi or an Uber is easy to call if I need one I know Metro can be a little intimidating for first time visitors especially if you're coming from a place that doesn't have any kind of Metro or Subway which is why I've tried really hard to make videos that help you get comfortable with the Metro System so that you know what's up before you arrive check out the description for a link to my Metro video playlist and check it out essential tip number five is to get over sticker shock Washington DC is an expensive city it just is is now exactly how severe your sticker shock is going to be depends heavily on where you live and what you're used to but being mad about the price of things here isn't going to make them go down it's just going to make you grumpy the place where most visitors will likely have sticker shock is at restaurants especially if you're thinking of ordering alcohol one thing you can do to alleviate this a bit is think about the types of restaurants you might want to visit and look look at their menus before your trip then create a budget and give yourself about 25% extra buffer and if you wind up being a little bit under budget even if you've still spent a lot it doesn't feel nearly as bad psychologically little tricks like this can actually really help reduce the stress that comes from sticker shock essential tip number six is to know exactly which tourist sites require tickets or reservations this one is especially important if you're planning to come here during Peak tourist season which is generally around Spring Break and summer break I'm recording this in March just before Peak spring break and every single Tour on the trip Hax DC calendar for the next several weeks is completely sold out with a weit list that keeps growing longer by the day this pains me because I know there are lots of people out there who waited too long to get their tickets and reservations and now they're going to miss out on some of the things they wanted to do many big tourist sites do not require tickets you just walk up and go but many of them do like some of the most popular Smithsonian museums the capital the Library of Congress the Washington Monument just to name a few so check the websites for all of the sites in advance and determine if you need a ticket and if you do get one as soon as you can essential tip number seven is to take tours and I know this one might sound a little self- serving because I am a tour guide but you know what I don't even care if you take one of my tours I rounded up a bunch of other great local tour companies and posted that list over at tripx dc.com looc tours I have no Financial incentive to recommend any of them these are all companies that are locally owned by people that I personally know and that I trust will show you a great time when I travel I personally sign up for a ton of Tours because I know they're the best way to experience a new place and I know it is very possible to visit many Washington DC tourist sites with no tour guide but just because I can cook food at home in my own kitchen doesn't make me a chef and it doesn't stop me from going out to restaurants when I want a special experience and in both cases a good tour or a good restaurant is not about sites or food it's about enhancing The Experience essential tip number eight is to think about your evening plans there are a ton of things to do in Washington DC as a tourist but most of them are admittedly daytime activities and this is a special true if you're a family traveling with kids who are obviously not going to do the traditional night life most museums and federal government sites close at 5 or 5:30 one of the most popular evening activities is to take a tour of the national monuments and memorials which makes sense as they are open 24 hours and kid-friendly other evening things you can do are sports concerts plays or shows think a little bit outside the box and consider things that aren't typically on the lists of standard tourist attractions essential tip number nine is to pack for the season while there are some things that I think should be in your suitcase no matter the time of year like comfortable walking shoes depending on what month you're going to be here you need to pack for the season visiting Washington DC in the winter is very different than coming here in the summer and what you packed should reflect that the good news for you is that I have an entire video where I go through what to pack and what I wear in all four seasons and I think you should go ahead and click or tap right over here and watch that now enjoy your trip

videos.transcript: Hey there fellow travelers, Mark and Rob, from TripHackDC. And today we're going to give you some budget travel advice when you're coming here to DC because it does not always mean a cheap vacation when you come to the nation's capital. Wouldn't you agree? It's a high cost city. I'm not going to lie about that. So what we're here today is going to go through accommodation tips, tour tips, sightseeing tips, food tips, stuff that's going to help you save some money when you do come here cuz you don't have to break the bank. It's very easy to break your bank here, but there's some things we need to do. And I think we need to start off by talking about accommodation. When you come really matters. If you're coming here when not a lot of people are around, you're going to get a good deal. If you're coming at a time when a lot of people are around, you're going to pay a premium. Yeah, I mean we're here in January and we were here a few years ago in July and I literally paid 1/3 of the price for my hotel room, same hotel, that I did 3 years ago because we're coming in off season. So that's a big thing. So one thing that can help you when you're looking for accommodation, no what no matter what time of the year coming, is really research different hotels and don't be kind of stuck thinking one hotel's always more expensive. Wouldn't you say? Yeah, a lot of it depends on events. If there's events happening, conferences, conventions, basketball tournaments, other things, a hotel might be completely booked because it is the official hotel of that event. And if you try to book there, it's going to be really expensive. So sometimes if you look nearby, look around, you can find other hotels that have a lot more availability and with more availability comes a little bit better rates. And then you can actually sometimes get a much nicer hotel for a more affordable price. And you're like, how is this possible? But that's why it's important to check around. Now, second thing we need to talk about is food because for as many free things as we'll talk about later is, when it comes to food, there's not a lot of cheap options. And so Rob, help me. Help me figure out how I can keep my manly figure when I'm here on a budget. Yeah, there's a lot of free activities, a lot of free museums in DC, but you're not going to eat for free. You've got to pay when you come. And so my favorite restaurants are what we call fast casual restaurants. And so, these are places that are better than fast food. These are above McDonald's or Taco Bell quality, right? But, they're not sit down, have a server come serve you, bring your drinks, all that stuff. And so, you know, most people probably know about Chipotle, for example. That's a fast casual restaurant. But, I also like places like Tacorian and Cava. Those are some places that are similar concept, but much more affordable than your sit down restaurant. Yeah, and I think another thing is important is when you're looking at going out to eat, sometimes the time of day can make a big difference. I know one of the things you helped me you hooked me up with last time I was here were the happy hours, right? Yeah, happy hours are much more affordable if you're going to be drinking, if you want to order beers and wines. You can get drinks at a happy hour sometimes for half the price that you would get them if you ordered them during dinner. I'm not going to sugarcoat it. Beers, wines, and especially cocktails during dinner are not cheap. And you're not going to find deals on that. If you want the deals, you can go to happy hour, which are typically, you know, before 7:00, 6:00, depending on which place you're going to. And that's where you're going to find the deals. And I think another thing, and you'll notice this when you're here, is if you're looking to cut costs when it comes to food, a lot of times it's breakfast is the easiest one to cut out. Yeah, I don't really even eat breakfast. I'm going to be honest about that. I always drink a cup of coffee. tell, breakfast, no breakfast. Yeah, I always I always get a cup of coffee and I might I might get a granola bar or something simple. And that's one thing you can do to save some money is if you're not a breakfast person, you can go to Starbucks and you could get a regular black coffee and you could get a little pastry or something there. Or if it's not Starbucks, a local coffee shop, and just get a little pastry and that's your breakfast. And that's going to be a lot less expensive than going to a restaurant. And also, we just don't really have a lot of breakfast restaurants, right? Now, brunch on the weekends, you can find places, right? You can get brunch anywhere. Every place that Any restaurant that's open is going to serve brunch on the weekends. But, it's not like we have a lot of diners, for example. Greasy spoons, that's kind of a relic of the past. We don't really have a lot of breakfast restaurants anymore. So, I like to cheap out on breakfast because it's the easiest one to. And the thing is is cheaping out on breakfast gets me thinking about there's stuff you can cheap out on, but I also think it's important that you kind of pick and choose a lot of things, whether it's food or tours or museums. And And like with restaurants for us, like my mom and I, we ate at a wonderful French restaurant last night, but for lunch today, we went to food courts and did that. And I think overall, I think it's important kind of think about picking and choosing when you should spend or don't spend. I mean, Rob's a tour guide. And And there's one thing I recommend spending money on is that actually getting a tour guide. I mean, I'll let you talk about this. This is your area, but Yeah. You can wander around and you can look at the sites. You can look at the capital from the outside and the Washington Monument and the Lincoln Memorial down on the other side of the park, and that's all completely free. And many people do that, and that's perfectly fine. But you could also sign up for a guided tour, and many people who come to DC say that that's often their most memorable experience. And when I travel, I'm not just saying this because I'm a tour guide, whenever I travel, I always sign up for tours, multiple tours usually during a trip. And one, if you're looking at food, the food tours, those could be fun, too, right? something I always do. When I'm in a new city, a city that I'm going to for the first time, I always sign up for a food tour, because that gives you a chance to taste all the local foods, the signature foods of that city. And it usually replaces at least one meal, if not a meal and a half. And so, it's it can actually save you a little bit of money because you don't have to get dinner that day you're going on the food tour. The food tour is your dinner. Exactly. That's a wonderful recommendation. And And you know, the tours take you around, but they're also we don't have to look at transport because the cheapest transportation that you're going to have in DC are your own two feet. That's right. Cuz this is a city of walky, walky, walky. My mom and I have been putting on about 9 mi a day walking around. And it's not like we haven't seen stuff, cuz you're going from place to place to place to place to place to place to place. And then in the museums, it's walky, walky, walky. So, you need good shoes, but you don't have to like Uber everywhere. You don't have to taxi everywhere. You can, but your two feet are the best things to have. Now, other helpful budget options for transportation if you don't want to do the walking are the Circulator bus. At the time of recording this, $1. The cheapest transportation in town. Circulator bus. I know a lot of people who come to DC, they love the Circulator bus because it's cheap and it's convenient and it gets you to places where you want to go. But, you could also do the metro if you needed to get a little bit farther away. Yep. So, then we have our fifth topic and that are the sites and the free sites to see. And here's the thing, there's tons of fantastic free sites to see here. And you know how you're going to learn about them? You're going to go click on that button over there and you're going to go watch Rob's video on 10 free places to see here in DC because the dude knows what he's talking about. Fantastic guy. Please tell him, Rob. Tell him where to find you. TripHackDC here on YouTube, triphackdc.com in your web browser. And I'm sure Mark will be generous enough to put a link to all of those things in the video description. And I will be honest, when we were talking about the tours before, a 3-hour tour with him for me saved me so much more time. I got so much more out of my trip, so that actually saved It cost me money, but it was worth the time. So, think about that as well. So, we'll see you later and bye from here in DC. Bye.

videos.title: Visiting Washington DC in 2026: What Tourists Need to Know
videos.description:
The 2026 peak travel season in Washington, DC is nearly here. This is a milestone year because it is America's 250th. If you're planning a visit there are some important updates to know about: tourist sites renovations and reopenings, transportation updates, plus a few sobering realities about the current state of life in Washington, DC.  

****************************************
~Trip Hacks DC resources for your trip~
Subscribe to the channel! - http://bit.ly/TripHacksDC  
Sign up for a guided tour - http://www.triphacksdc.com/tours 
Trip Hacks DC PODCAST - https://www.triphacksdc.com/podcast
Recommended Hotels Guide - https://www.triphacksdc.com/hotels
Contact us about tours - https://www.triphacksdc.com/contact-us 

****************************************
~Resources from this video~
America's 250th podcast episode - https://www.triphacksdc.com/podcast-dc250/
Metro contactless payments video - https://youtu.be/cNhm9D2RJN4
Crystal City station closure info - https://www.wmata.com/about/news/Crystal-City-construction-update-Shuttles-replace-trains-to-airport-and-nearby-stations-for-10-weekends.cfm

****************************************
~News articles referenced in this video~
NPS fountain work - https://www.nps.gov/nama/learn/news/fountainwork.htm
Elephant calf born at the zoo - https://nationalzoo.si.edu/news/female-asian-elephant-calf-born-smithsonians-national-zoo-and-conservation-biology-institute
DC unemployment highest in the US - https://www.yahoo.com/news/articles/dc-unemployment-rate-highest-us-181855519.html
Boycott of US travel shows no sign of slowing - https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/ce9d7pj543eo
D.C. tourism was already struggling. Then the National Guard arrived. - https://www.washingtonpost.com/travel/2025/08/29/dc-tourism-trump-takeover-national-guard-impacts/
Another Victim of the Shutdown: Tourism in the Nation’s Capital - https://www.nytimes.com/2025/11/07/travel/washington-dc-travel-shutdown.html
Record number of D.C. restaurants will close this year - https://www.axios.com/local/washington-dc/2025/12/19/dc-restaurant-closures-2025-record-number
Trump plans to close Kennedy Center for about two years - https://www.washingtonpost.com/style/2026/02/01/kennedy-center-trump-closure-construction/
Kennedy Center ticket sales have plummeted since Trump takeover - https://www.washingtonpost.com/entertainment/2025/10/31/kennedy-center-sales/

****************************************
~Follow Trip Hacks DC on social media~
https://www.instagram.com/triphacksdc  
https://www.facebook.com/triphacksdc
https://www.tiktok.com/@triphacksdc

****************************************
~Video Chapters~
0:00 Washington DC renovations and 2026 reopenings 
7:04 Public White House tours
9:21 Washington DC transportation updates
12:21 Real talk: the current state of Washington DC
15:24 Kennedy Center shutdown
tags: things to do in washington dc
videos.transcript: If you're planning to visit Washington DC in 2026, I have a lot of important information to tell you about. But first, hello. My name is Rob. I'm a tour guide and founder of Trip Hacks DC Tours. And on this YouTube channel, I share my best tips, tricks, and travel hacks for visitors who want to explore Washington DC. The most notable thing about 2026 is that this year is America's 250th. July 4th will be exactly 250 years since our founding fathers signed and delivered the Declaration of Independence to the British Crown. A lot of tourist sites around here have spent the last year or more getting spruced up in anticipation of this anniversary and a few are still works in progress. The major site that I think many people will be happy to hear about is the National Air and Space Museum on the National Mall, which itself is celebrating its 50th birthday as it opened in 1976. Air and Space has been under some degree of renovation for about the past decade, but it is finally wrapping up. If you enter the museum on the north side, the National Mall side, you will find a beautiful new entrance and newer, faster metal detectors. Once inside, you'll see that even more galleries have reopened. More will be reopening in 2026, and a few might spill over into 2027. The IMAX movie theater and the Planetarium have both reopened. This one is exciting for me as this IMAX theater is one of my favorite places in all of Washington DC to see movies. Nearby on the National Mall, the Smithsonian Castle is currently closed, but will be temporarily reopening from Memorial Day weekend through Labor Day weekend and will have special 250th programming. I'm actually not going to go very in-depth on all of the 250th programming in this video because I just published a much more in-depth podcast episode with lots of information, including the special events that are scheduled specifically for the 250th, information about events that happen annually but have been remarketed to feature the 250th, and information about new museum museum exhibits that are specifically focused on the 250th or on American history. Plus, in that podcast episode, I have some early predictions about the 4th of July. You can listen in Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or on the Trip Hacks DC website or whatever your favorite podcast app is. And I will leave a link to that episode in the video description. If you're planning on visiting museums in DC this year, most of them do not require any reservations or tickets, but a few do, including the Air and Space Museum, the National Museum of African-American History and Culture, and the National Zoo. Tickets are free, but you do need them, so make sure to go on the official Smithsonian website and get them in advance. Speaking of the zoo, the National Zoo just announced this month that a baby elephant has been born and is joining the herd. The National Zoo may not be the biggest zoo in the world, but between the newish pandas and the new baby elephant, those are a few compelling reasons to check it out. Mount Vernon, George Washington's home, is another major tourist site that has mostly reopened. It was under heavy renovation last year, and most of the mansion was unavailable when you visited. Now, they've moved into the final phase of renovation, which means that most of the mansion is back open, but the seller is still closed. That final phase of renovation should wrap up later this year. The National Geographic Museum is also reopening in 2026 after a multi-year renovation. I've always liked this one, and it's been one of my favorite private museums. Back on the National Mall, work at the Lincoln Memorial is ongoing. The Lincoln Memorial has been under heavy construction for the past several years as they finish building a brand new museum in the Undercraftoft. The plan is to have this new museum open by summer along with infrastructure improvements like a new store and new restrooms and accessibility improvements including two new elevators. And once those new elevators are operational, the big accessibility ramp that's currently out front will come down. The tidal basin seaw wall project is also coming along. Construction of the seaw wall itself is now complete and they've moved into the landscaping portion of the project. Maybe you can see behind me, but the fences are still up around the perimeter. So, I don't know whether we'll be able to walk on the new paths they've laid this spring for the cherry blossom festival. But even if we can, it's going to take a few years for them to plant the replacement cherry trees here and for them to fill out the space. Speaking of fences, the National Park Service sent out a news release about a month ago about 18 water fountains and water features in DC that they plan to restore or renovate. Some of them are already fenced off, like the Korean War Memorial and the Columbus Memorial outside of Union Station. Others on the list haven't been touched yet. National Park Service also sent out a second news release that same day that said the World War II Memorial was completely closing for several months. This was quite concerning to me as a multi-month closure of a major memorial would be a big deal for a lot of tourists, especially in peak spring season. But for whatever reason, that news release was not accurate. I went to the World War II memorial this week and no construction had even started. And I was able to confirm with National Park Service that even once construction begins, they will not be closing the entire memorial. So that's a bit of a relief. But the bottom line for visitors this spring is that you should expect at least some of the National Monuments memorials to be fenced off to some degree and under construction. But which ones? on which timeline? That's still a big question mark. Now, let's talk about White House tours. I've been getting a lot of questions about these lately, and unfortunately, there is a lot of conflicting information floating around out there, especially from AI chat bots, which as an aside, you should all be way more skeptical about trusting to plan your trips. White House tours were very on and off last year. In August 2025, White House tours were suspended indefinitely so that they could bring in the wrecking balls and demolish the East Wing. For the next several months, there were zero public tours of the White House. In December 2025, White House tours resumed with a re-imagined tour route. What is a re-imagined route? It's a shorter route with fewer things to see. I want to make this crystal clear because even before last August, people often had wildly unrealistic expectations about what they were going to get to see on the public tour of the White House. Before the demolition, the John Q public tour of the White House did not include any of the famous rooms in the West Wing, and it still doesn't. post demolition. It also doesn't include any rooms in the east wing because there is no more east wing. And it no longer includes any of the rooms on the ground floor like the map room or the library because those rooms have been repurposed as offices for East Wing staff that were displaced by the demolition. A regular public tour of the White House currently has about 50% fewer rooms to see than even before on an already pretty limited tour. Now, if you're a billionaire or a CEO, you shouldn't have any trouble getting into the White House right now. But for the rest of us, regular Joe's public tours of the White House must be requested through a member of Congress's office. I think most visitors who are interested in the White House from a historical or an educational perspective are better served going to a museum like the people's house which is run by the nonprofit White House historical association. Now let's run through some DC transportation updates. Like the Air and Space Museum, Metro Rail is celebrating its 50th birthday this year. Metro first started rail service in March 1976. So, happy birthday, Metro. You can now use contactless payments on both Metro Rail and Metro Bus. Contactless payments means you can use a credit or a debit card to ride, and you don't need a Smart Trip card if you don't want one. I made an entire tutorial video about contactless payments on Metro Rail, so make sure to check that out if you haven't seen it yet and want to learn. The process is the same on Metro Bus with the exception that on Metro Rail you tap in and out of the system, whereas on Metro Bus, you only tap once when you board. Metro also finally has an official app. It's called Metro Pulse, and you can get it in either the Apple App Store or Google Play Store. Unlike most Metro apps that have existed throughout history, this one is officially supported by Metro, and it includes both Metro Rail and Metro Bus. You can still use thirdparty apps if you like. My go-to has always been Metro Hero, but as it's a volunteer supported app, its long-term viability is not guaranteed. I usually don't talk too much about Metro weekend track work, but there is one coming up that tourists will want to know about. On 10 weekends starting this weekend and running through June, the Crystal City station will be closed and there will be no trains running through. This means there will be a gap in rail service between Pentagon City and National Airport. This is important because it means if you're flying in or out of National Airport on one of the weekends when this work is happening, Metro may not be the most convenient option for getting downtown. It's also important because I know some visitors prefer a hotel in Crystal City rather than one downtown and I would suggest perhaps reconsidering that if your stay overlaps with one of the weekends when the Crystal City station is closed. Specific details are available on wamada.com and I will leave a link to the announcement in the video description. Another not so great transportation update is that the DC street car is discontinuing service in 2026. I know a lot of tourists might not have even known that DC has a street car because it's in an area that's more used by locals than by visitors. But last year we lost the DC circulator bus which was beloved by tourists and now the street car. Losing cheap or in the case of the street car, free transportation options is a bit of a blow. And with that, now we get into the more sobering portion of this video. We need to have some real talk about the current state of Washington DC. And to be clear, the reason I'm including this in the video is because I think it's important context for a visit. It's important to understand when you visit why the vibes feel the way that they do, why the local mood may not feel so great, and why it might be different than what you remember from a trip in the past. It's important so that you understand when you visit why many locals are not particularly jazzed about America's 250th, even if that's your primary motivation for visiting. And it's important because I hope I can inspire some empathy and that you can pack some patience when you visit, knowing that a lot of folks here are going through some stuff. To put it bluntly, DC is down bad and has been really hurting for the past year. The local economy is in tough shape and keeps taking one hit after another. It all started last winter with Elon Musk's chainsaw of cuts and mass firings of civil servants. Whatever you philosophically believe about the size of the federal government, the way that these layoffs were conducted was chaos to the extreme. They created a lot of uncertainty and a lot of unemployment in a very short period of time. And as a result, DC now has one of the worst unemployment situations in the entire country. Everybody here has either personally suffered a job loss or knows at least someone pretty close to them who has. And even for those who didn't directly lose a job, many were still caught up in the chaos that ensued from these cuts. Tourism took a hit last year. First in the spring when international visitors collectively decided they weren't going to travel to the US anymore. Then in the summer because of the National Guard deployment which damaged the city's reputation and painted DC as an undesirable place to visit. Then in the fall with the historically long government shutdown which closed museums and major tourist sites and caused many people to miss multiple paychecks. Restaurants and small businesses are struggling. DC had a record number of restaurant closures in 2025 and I can say anecdotally that there are more vacant and empty former restaurant spaces in my neighborhood than pretty much ever before. It has just been hit after hit after hit. Which brings us to the final major tourism update, which is that the president of the United States wants to shut down the Kennedy Center this summer. In a social media post, he wrote that he wants to shut the Kennedy Center for a 2-year renovation starting on July 4th in honor of America's 250th. I'm sorry, I'm probably going to crash out over this at least a little bit, but this Kennedy Center news really feels like the breaking point. It feels like the symbolic culmination of everything that DC has gone through over the past year. I know there are bigger problems in the world right now, but I am genuinely upset about the Kennedy Center, and I cannot pretend like any of this is fine or just a normal tourism update. One year ago, I was strategizing about getting tickets for Hamilton at the Kennedy Center as part of America's 250th. Now, not only is Hamilton not coming to DC at all, but the entire Kennedy Center is shuttering. In October, the Washington Post published a deep dive investigation about how ticket sales at the Kennedy Center have plummeted following the president's unprecedented takeover. The Kennedy Center functioned just fine from 2017 through 2021. By pretty much every metric, it was the unprecedented takeover last year that destroyed the business model of the center. The president says we need to shut it down for 2 years to renovate. The Kennedy Center just had a $250 million renovation in anticipation of its 50th anniversary, which we celebrated a few years ago. Trump posted on social media that the Kennedy Center is tired, broken, and dilapidated. The Kennedy Center is an amazing space. I've seen great performances and made amazing memories at the Kennedy Center. I feel for the artists who are caught up in this. I feel for the visitors and tourists who are no longer able to experience what was once a great institution. I feel for the ushers and the box office staff and the stage hands and the technicians and everyone who learned via tweet that they are losing their jobs. I feel for all the people who never signed up for any of this. The closing of the Kennedy Center is a loss of art and culture. Yes, but it's also another mass layoff that the local economy doesn't need. I don't really have a happy ending to this video, so I'll just say if you made it this far, thanks for sticking around with me. Wow. And if you're planning to visit this year, if I could just make one ask, it's please support local businesses, local restaurants, tour companies, coffee shops, boutiques, whatever. It may not seem like a lot, but it matters.

safetyNotes: So, I don't know whether we'll be able to walk on the new paths they've laid this spring for the cherry blossom festival
Contactless payments means you can use a credit or a debit card to ride, and you don't need a Smart Trip card if you don't want one
I usually don't talk too much about Metro weekend track work, but there is one coming up that tourists will want to know about
I don't really have a happy ending to this video, so I'll just say if you made it this far, thanks for sticking around with me

videos.transcript: the best tip I can give you for driving in Washington DC is don't if you're visiting from out of town driving around here can be a stressful and frustrating experience the good news is that there are tons of different ways of getting around DC from Metro to Capital Bike Share to walking so the airport rental car which is necessary in many destinations is not required here and if you're curious to learn about every single one of those Transportation options and my opinions about them I have a video on this exact topic by the way hello my name is Rob I'm a tour guide and the founder of trip hex DC tours on this channel I share my best tips tricks and hacks for visitors who want to explore Washington DC and I'm curious to know if you've been to DC before what's your preferred way of getting around leave a comment down there and let me know my second tip is avoid driving in rush hour whenever possible rush hour traffic in DC is heavy and unpredictable and you might be thinking since covid and everyone started working from home is Rush Hour even still a thing it's true that many white collar office workers are still working at least part-time at home however the answer to the question is yes rush hour is still a thing the unfortunate reality is for whatever their reasons lots of people started driving more during covid and taking Metro less so there may be fewer people working downtown than pre-covered but because driving is so much less efficient than taking Public Transportation traffic still feels gridlocked during rush hour tip number three is avoid driving on the weekends but wait you might be thinking rob you just told us not to drive during rush hour and shouldn't weekends be more chill and the truth is that weekend traffic is often worse than rush hour traffic for several reasons the first is that people who live in the suburbs nearby off often drive into the city on the weekends to have fun and explore people who live in the city are driving all around doing errands and then you have special events festivals marathons that are almost always on the weekend special events marathons Etc are almost always accompanied by Big Time road closures which makes driving a lot more challenging for example we recently had a big beer festival called snallygaster and it was held right in the middle of Pennsylvania Avenue which met several blocks of what is otherwise a main thoroughfare were closed so for all of these reasons driving on the weekend often has lots of challenges tip number four is know exactly where you're going to park now I hear people say sometimes that there is nowhere to park or that it's impossible to find a space and that's not really true there are actually lots of places to park but there are very few free places to park if you're going to drive in DC a huge mistake is assuming that you're going to find a free place to park directly in front of your destination that sort of thing may be the reality in the suburbs where you live but it's not the case here in the city there are garages near the main tourist sites like at Union Station City Center and The Wharf but you're going to have to pay and if you're used to free the price is probably going to seem like a lot but that's the cost of the privilege of driving in DC which leads to tip number five which is be very careful with street parking another piece of not very good advice that I hear locals give tourists sometimes is just park on the street street parking is tricky some of it is metered some of it is time limited some of it is metered and time limited and while Washington DC is generally a safe place for visitors the one place you do need to be vigilant is with your car I really don't want anyone's trip ruined because something happened to their car while it was getting parked on a street a related tip number six is don't take parking advice from people who have no idea what they're talking about it's wild to me how often someone will walk up to me and ask if it's okay to leave their car where they parked it like I'm the Emperor of parking or something one time a guy parked his family minivan in front of this sign he walked up to me and asked is this okay I looked at the sign and said well you can park there but not legally he then said thanks locked up and walked off to take his family to see the sights now what the guy was really wanting me to say was ah yeah you're fine don't worry about it and lots of people would have told him that but like this is your vehicle do you really want to deal with the worst case scenario if it comes to that let's talk about what could have happened to this guy first if you're illegally parked you could get a ticket if you get a bunch of tickets or you've visited before and gotten tickets that you never paid well then you could get a boot or if you leave your car in a tow away Zone like on a busy street during rush hour you could get tow it away now is this really likely to happen does it matter the last place I would ever want to spend a vacation is at an impound lot because I was trying to cheap out with street parking and tip number seven is be careful driving in DC is challenging for a lot of reasons there are one-way streets there are traffic circles but the thing you need to watch out for more than anything else is other drivers now I know that everybody thinks the drivers are the worst wherever they live I don't really think it's a competition I think driving in the U.S nationally has gotten worse since covid traffic deaths nationally hit a record high in 2021 it's tragic and anecdotally I've noticed that the amount of reckless driving around here it's gone up and besides that in DC there are speed cameras there are red light cameras if you break the law there is a greater than zero chance you're going to get busted for it so be careful out there and hey if you made it this far then I highly recommend watching another trip hacks DC Video so go ahead and click or tap right over here right there right now enjoy your trip
